author: @flightofthejellyfish
text:
HDPE is also milk jugs as a common source and hard hats if you need a thicker bit. I don't have a 3d printer but I'm pretty sure you can get HDPE filament, you can certainly get nylon which is the next more slippery plastic second only to Teflon.
I am wondering if the graphite treatment would be the go for all your printed cogs and things.

text: Would 2 doughnut magnets ( holes in the middle to mount underneath the rotor ) with similar poles opposing one another interfere with the magnetic fields of the serpent coil sir?  Due to proximity?

author: @12thsonofisrael
text:
There's a 'plastic-like' material called UHMW Ultra High Molecular Wear, almost as tough as steel that is used instead of bearings. 

Bore a hole that's just large enough for the load bearing shaft and use it just like any other bearing, runs slick and smooth. 

I've seen it in use on conveyors that runs 24/7, seemed to never wear out.

author: @thehazelnutspread
text: if you glue magnets to the stand and to the bottom of the fan, they will cause the fan to hover above the stand. That should result in ZERO friction.

author: @andylewis7360

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text:
One thing I like to use is bicycle wheel ballbearings, because I just have bags of them from the amount of back axles I used to get through.
If you subtract a torus from being sandwiched between two cylinders meant to fit on the shaft, with a small gap between them already, you could make a ring bearing where one just sits on top of the other on the shaft with the ballbearings inbetween.

author: @ile84
text: I guess you could've just rub that graphite straigth into the printed plastic parts. I mean carbon (graphite) is a dry lubricant as is magnesium oxide (talcum powder). Now you can try it out which is better, with that extra plastic or without.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: In some of my wind turbines I've used a shaft riding on a single steel ball , in a bath of oil, all inside of a brass nipple (pipe). Spins like a childs top. Thanks to Marcus Goeller for that invention.

author: @PhilWaud
text: This is a great project, loving how its developing. I thought you were going to suggest a magnetic bearing, surely two rings of magnets with similar poles facing inwards mounted to the turbine blade and the coil side would result in a very low friction bearing that requires no maintenance. It also increases the mass of the turbine which should help?

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
Years ago I was trying to think of a way to make a simple frictionless bearing. It was more a thought experiment. What I came up with was magnets.  
Vertical axis: Five (if the diameter of the axial equals the magnets diameter) with the same pole facing up on the base and five with the same pole facing down on rotating unit. 
Horizontal axis: use a bar magnet for the axial. At the north end of the axial use the north pole of the spinning magnets facing the axial. South with south.
I never tried either so this is just an idea

author: @shanealexander9952
text:
For balancing riding lawnmower blades, I took a slightly used spindle with the bearing still in it.  Too much drag to balance blades.  But I carefully degreased the bearing and lightly used sewing machine oil.  Eventually I used powered graphite with no oil in the bearing.  That made such freedom of movement I fixed factory blades easily.  Way better than commercial balancing at the time.  
Why? It was a lawn business and sharp blades made for fast cutting.  One sharp pair every day and less vibration.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
I'd always clean the blade spotless, including rust, and use a 1/8th inch piece of brass tube as a balance.
Hand filing, it took forever, but, I only sharpened my blade once or twice a year, depending on rocks.

replies.author: @shanealexander9952
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 30 hours a week on other peoples lawns.   Rocks, limbs, toys, wires...Every 2 days x3 blades, if nothing else just to keep vibration down.  Worked great with an angle grinder.  Could cleanly mow as fast as the mower would go.  It became a huge sales point.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: @shanealexander9952 Oh, you don't need to sell me on the values of a sharp blade. I worked in trees for many years.

author: @Flashahol
text:
interesting, very interesting...
Most people think about lubricants as liquid or grease (grease is not so much a lubricant as a heat and dust absorber or rust preservative most of the time), but few know that there are solid lubricants such as graphite. Didn't expect it to grab so well onto plastic, though... interesting! Perhaps some preliminary light sanding could enhance it (or make it worse).

author: @russellzauner
text:
Disclaimer: not an affiliate of any supplier
Igus makes tribological materials - some are available in spools of filament so you can print your own parts.  I got a spool for our lab and made all sorts of little parts that removed the need to lubricate some things and improved the performance of others.  It's pretty cool stuff, they also make powder (for sintering), bar stock (for milling), and tapes (for making surfaces low friction/wear), and also have a service where you can send them a drawing for a quote on just building it for you, if you like.
It's kind of a soup to nuts vendor, starting with proprietary materials and all the way to fabrication.  They have other interesting products but the important thing is they're affordable and accessible, always willing to help and will return your calls even if you're not some business - they seem to enjoy supporting interesting projects even if there is no sales volume in it for them.

author: @joserobles11
text:
May I suggest graphite-graphite bearings? It might be the best coeficient possible. So would be two graphite discs (one for each moving part)
Also maybe at low speeds graphite gets more stuck so maybe by cutting the disc so that there is only a strip in the outter part it will gain speed quickly for each revolution rather than in the inner part.
Lastly i would take care about the "flatness" since any subtle warp would make the efficiency go down like crazy
Hope it helps! Cheers and keep it up guys!

author: @user-hf3ym7lh4d
text: Wonder how it compares to using graphite lube directly without the HDPE disc. Or other lubricants, for that matter. What'd be even more awesome is if it were shaped as an air bearing straight off the 3d printer; no lube and almost no friction besides wind drag. At least until it gets leaves and bugs stuck in it, but you'll have to deal with those same maintenance issues in the small moving feathers of the turbine anyway.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: Not PTFE but  HDPE+Graphite

replies.author: @user-hf3ym7lh4d

author: @nirodha7028
text: Makes no sense to have the surface area causing friction to be all the way to the outer edge… the arm of the friction force vector is needlessly long and causes a bigger than needed negative momentum around the central axis. Use the same method but have the friction surfaces be much closer to the centre. Also: hang the entire device upside down (rework the bearings to suit that position) and see it will perform with greater efficiency due to the flaps always being in optimum position for the wind to catch them ‘unless’ they are on the non effective side and get blown upwards by the wind. 😊

replies.author: @JonLovestheLord
replies.text: Simplicity to make is the key.  By using a larger diameter the area is increased dramatically and it prevents heating which would soften and perhaps even melt the HDPE substrate.  I'm sure there is an easy way to figure out the optimal diameter.  As to upside down flaps, I would suspect that the turbulent flow would increase dramatically causing excess unwanted drag from having to move multiple vanes out of the wind path, don't you think?  It would be interesting to see some side by side comparisons.

replies.author: @nirodha7028
replies.text: @JonLovestheLord Heating of the surface would only occur noticably with high friction and high rpm. A bearing near the centre would be much more efficient and lower drag (allow for longer freespin). Developer himself mentioned this is fun and games for a quick and dirty model. Not for end use. With regards to the flaps: there might be unexpected results but my gut feeling sais upside down will work better (with the proper bearing changes to make that possible)

author: @grendel1960a
text: milk bottles (the plastic ones- not the glass kind) also make good low friction bearings. cut out a washer from the milk bottle and you get a good low friction bearing, you can also use small pads of milk bottle around the circumference of a larger disk. we used to do this for a low friction base for large dobsonian telescopes (think 24" diameter lazy susan bearing.) nb these are also HDPE

author: @DurpVonFronz
text: I know the bearing inside of computer fans kind of have a spot where they almost keep moving on their own, like an offset or w/e its strange to watch em slow down, they start jumping.

replies.author: @johndough8115
replies.text: High quality PC fans use ball bearings.  Others use standard brass bearings... and the fan itself is partially suspended by the magnetic fields.  You can feel the suspension, by pressing the fan center downwards.. and it will spring back up.   There is also the resistance you will feel from the alternating magnetic gates / poles.

replies.author: @DurpVonFronz
replies.text: @johndough8115 Ahhh, thats whats going on with them, I knew they acted strangely but never knew why. Thanks for filling me in on that :)
